parts.content:
The 'EduGlobal' portal must be accessible to students using various browsers (e.g., Chrome, Safari, Firefox) and different operating systems. 

Explain the role of the **World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)** in making this possible.
parts.markscheme:
- The W3C develops and maintains common protocols and standards (e.g., HTML5, CSS) <marks>1</marks>
- These standards ensure interoperability between different web browsers and hardware <marks>1</marks>
- By following W3C recommendations, developers can ensure that their code is interpreted consistently across platforms <marks>1</marks>
- This promotes a universal web that is accessible to all users regardless of their chosen technology/device <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award up to [4 max]</note>

<totalMarks>Total 4 marks</totalMarks>

parts.content:
The portal consists of a public blog and a private database of academic papers that requires a subscription to access.

Distinguish between the **Surface Web** and the **Deep Web** in the context of this portal, and explain why the academic papers might not appear in general search engine results.
parts.markscheme:
- The Surface Web consists of pages that are publicly accessible and have been indexed by traditional search engines <marks>1</marks>
- In this scenario, the public blog belongs to the Surface Web as it is linked and crawlable <marks>1</marks>
- The Deep Web refers to parts of the web whose contents are not indexed by standard search engines <marks>1</marks>
- The database of academic papers is part of the Deep Web because it is hidden behind an authentication layer (login/password) <marks>1</marks>
- Search engine crawlers cannot bypass paywalls, fill out forms, or access password-protected directories, meaning this content remains unindexed <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award [2 max] for distinguishing the terms, [2 max] for the application to the scenario, and [1] for a clear explanation of why the papers are not indexed.</note>

<totalMarks>Total 5 marks</totalMarks>

questions.specification: A university website uses HTTPS for secure communications.

parts.content: Define the term HTTPS.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) as an extension of HTTP that uses encryption for secure communication over a computer network. <marks>1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the full name
steps.content:
To define HTTPS using the standard functional definition, we first identify the acronym. HTTPS stands for **Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure**.

This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic 3.1: Networks** and **Topic C.1: Web Science**.

steps.title: Define its functional role
steps.content:
The functional role of HTTPS is to act as a secure extension of the standard HTTP protocol. While HTTP sends data in plain text, HTTPS ensures that communication between a web browser and a server is protected.

You can think of it as the "secure version" of the protocol used to load web pages.

highlights.text: HTTPS

steps.title: Explain the security mechanism
steps.content: The "Secure" part of the definition comes from its use of encryption. HTTPS utilizes encryption protocols (such as TLS or SSL) to encode data during transfer. This prevents unauthorized users (like hackers) from reading or modifying the information being sent, such as passwords or credit card details.

steps.title: Construct the final definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, combine these elements into a single concise sentence:

**HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure)** is an extension of HTTP that utilizes encryption to provide secure communication and data transfer over a computer network.

parts.content: Explain two reasons why HTTPS is important for protecting user data on websites.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** encryption: HTTPS encrypts the data being sent between the user and the server <marks>1</marks>, which ensures that sensitive information (such as login credentials) remains unreadable to unauthorized third parties even if intercepted. <marks>1</marks>

- **Explain** authentication: The server's identity is verified using a digital certificate <marks>1</marks>, ensuring that the user is interacting with the legitimate university website and not a fraudulent or malicious impersonator. <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award 2 marks for each well-explained reason, up to a maximum of 4 marks. Other valid points include data integrity (preventing unauthorized modification of data during transit).</note>

steps.title: Understand HTTPS basics
steps.content: To answer this, we need to look at how HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) adds a layer of security over standard HTTP. In the IB Computer Science syllabus, specifically **Topic 3.1: Networks**, we learn that HTTPS uses the SSL/TLS protocol to provide two main security features: **encryption** and **authentication**.

steps.title: Explain data encryption
steps.content:
The first major reason is **encryption**. HTTPS scrambles the data sent between the university student's browser and the server. 

Even if a malicious actor intercepts the packets during transmission, the information will be unreadable 'ciphertext' rather than plain text. This is crucial for protecting sensitive data like login credentials or personal student records.

highlights.text: HTTPS encrypts the data being sent between the user and the server
sensitive information (such as login credentials) remains unreadable to unauthorized third parties even if intercepted.

steps.title: Explain server authentication
steps.content:
The second reason is **authentication**. HTTPS uses **digital certificates** to verify that the university's website is legitimate. 

When your browser connects via HTTPS, it checks a certificate issued by a trusted third party. This ensures you are actually communicating with the university's official server and not a fraudulent website (phishing) trying to steal your information.

highlights.text: The server's identity is verified using a digital certificate
ensuring that the user is interacting with the legitimate university website and not a fraudulent or malicious impersonator.

steps.title: Review the mark allocation
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks by linking the technology to the benefit:

1.  **Encryption** (1 mark) $\rightarrow$ Resulting in **data being unreadable** if intercepted (1 mark).
2.  **Digital Certificates** (1 mark) $\rightarrow$ Resulting in **verification of the legitimate website** (1 mark).

steps.title: Identify the role of HTTPS
steps.content:
HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) is the standard protocol for secure communication over a network. It combines HTTP with the SSL/TLS protocol to protect data using two main pillars of security: **Encryption** and **Data Integrity**.

In the context of a university website, this protects sensitive information like student grades, financial details, and login credentials from being stolen or altered.

highlights.text: A university website uses HTTPS for secure communications.

steps.title: Reason 1: Data Encryption
steps.content:
The first reason HTTPS is vital is **Encryption**. 

HTTPS encrypts the data being sent between the user's browser and the university server. This transforms plain text data into **ciphertext**, which is unreadable without the correct decryption key. 

**How it earns marks:**
*   **Mark 1:** State that HTTPS encrypts the data being transmitted.
*   **Mark 2:** Explain that this ensures sensitive information (like passwords) remains unreadable to unauthorized third parties even if they intercept the packets.

steps.title: Reason 2: Data Integrity
steps.content:
The second reason is **Data Integrity**, which ensures that the information hasn't been changed during transit.

HTTPS uses mechanisms like hashing (digital signatures) to verify that the data arriving at the destination is exactly the same as the data sent by the source. If an attacker tries to modify a student's grade or a course application form while it is traveling across the network, the system will detect the mismatch and reject the data.

**How it earns marks:**
*   **Mark 3:** State that HTTPS prevents unauthorized modification of data during transit.
*   **Mark 4:** Explain that this guarantees the accuracy and reliability of the data received by the university or the user.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To get full marks for this question, you need to provide two distinct reasons and explain the benefit of each:

1.  **Encryption:** Scrambles data so it is useless to interceptors ($2$ marks).
2.  **Data Integrity:** Uses checksums/hashes to ensure data is not altered or corrupted ($2$ marks).

*Note: You could also mention **Authentication** (using digital certificates to verify the website's identity) as an alternative valid reason!*

parts.content: Describe how SSL certificates are involved in HTTPS security.
parts.markscheme:
- **Describe** identity verification: SSL certificates are issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA) to verify the authenticity of the website owner. <marks>1</marks>

- **Describe** encryption keys: The certificate contains the website's public key, which is used during the initial 'handshake' to establish a secure, encrypted connection between the browser and the server. <marks>1</marks>

- **Describe** trust indicators: Browsers validate the certificate to ensure it is current and from a trusted source, typically displaying a padlock icon to signify to the user that the connection is secure. <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

<examTip>Make sure to mention both the technical role (encryption keys) and the trust/verification aspects of SSL certificates.</examTip>

steps.title: Identity verification via CA
steps.content: The first functional role of an SSL certificate is **authentication**. Before a university can use HTTPS, a third-party organization called a **Certificate Authority (CA)** must issue them a certificate. This process involves the CA verifying that the university truly owns and operates the website. This prevents 'impersonation' attacks, where a malicious user might try to trick students into visiting a fake login page.

highlights.text: SSL certificates are issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA) to verify the authenticity of the website owner.

steps.title: Public keys for encryption
steps.content: Technically, the certificate acts as a container for the website’s **public key**. When a browser connects to the university site, the server sends this certificate. The browser uses the public key during the initial 'handshake' to securely exchange data needed to create an encrypted tunnel. Because only the university server has the matching **private key**, only that server can decrypt the information sent by the browser, ensuring data privacy.

highlights.text: The certificate contains the website's public key, which is used during the initial 'handshake' to establish a secure, encrypted connection

steps.title: Visual validation for users
steps.content: Finally, the certificate provides **visual trust indicators** for the end-user. The web browser automatically checks the certificate's details, such as its expiration date and whether it was signed by a trusted CA. If the certificate is valid, the browser displays a **padlock icon** in the address bar. This tells the user that their connection to the university website is private and the site's identity has been confirmed.

highlights.text: typically displaying a padlock icon to signify to the user that the connection is secure.

steps.title: Initiating the handshake
steps.content: When a user connects to a secure university website, the process begins with the **HTTPS handshake**. The browser sends a request to the server asking for identification to ensure the connection is safe before any sensitive data is exchanged.

steps.title: Identity verification via CA
steps.content: The server responds by sending its **SSL certificate**. This certificate is issued by a **Certificate Authority (CA)**, which is a trusted third-party organization. The browser checks this certificate to verify that the website owner is who they claim to be, preventing "man-in-the-middle" attacks where an impostor might try to intercept the data.

highlights.text: SSL certificates are issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA) to verify the authenticity of the website owner.

steps.title: Exchanging the public key
steps.content: The SSL certificate also contains the server's **public key**. During the handshake, the browser uses this public key to encrypt information that will be used to create a unique **session key**. Because only the university's server has the matching private key to decrypt this, a secure, encrypted tunnel is established for the session.

highlights.text: The certificate contains the website's public key, which is used during the initial 'handshake' to establish a secure, encrypted connection

steps.title: Confirming the secure connection
steps.content: Once the browser has validated the certificate as current and trusted, it completes the handshake. To signify this to the user, the browser typically displays a **padlock icon** in the address bar. This is a visual trust indicator confirming that all data sent to the university is now encrypted and secure.

highlights.text: typically displaying a padlock icon to signify to the user that the connection is secure.

questions.specification: A developer is building a web portal for an open-source documentation project. The portal needs to be highly accessible, performant, and easily indexed by search engines to ensure that users can find technical guides efficiently.

parts.content:
The developer decides to adhere strictly to the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) standards for HTML and CSS.

Outline **three** reasons why adhering to these web standards is beneficial for a web project.
parts.markscheme:
### Benefits of adhering to W3C standards

- **Interoperability/Cross-browser compatibility**: Standards ensure that the website renders and functions consistently across different web browsers and devices. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Accessibility**: Using standardized semantic HTML (e.g., correct use of `<nav>`, `<header>`, and `alt` tags) ensures that screen readers and other assistive technologies can interpret the content for users with disabilities. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Search Engine Optimization (SEO)**: Search engine crawlers can more easily parse and index well-structured, valid code, potentially improving the site's ranking. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Maintenance and Future-proofing**: Standardized code is easier to maintain and more likely to remain compatible with future browser versions or new technologies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Reduced Bandwidth**: Clean, standardized code often results in smaller file sizes, which reduces server load and decreases page loading times. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each reason outlined up to [3 max].</note>

parts.content:
To manage how search engines interact with the portal, the developer includes both a `robots.txt` file and an XML sitemap.

Explain how these **two** files assist search engines in the indexing process.
parts.markscheme:
### How robots.txt and sitemaps assist indexing

- The `robots.txt` file provides instructions to web crawlers about which directories or pages should **not** be crawled (e.g., private admin folders), saving the crawler's "crawl budget." <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The XML sitemap acts as a roadmap of the website, listing all the URLs that the developer wants to be indexed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Together, they ensure that the crawler finds all relevant content (via the sitemap) while avoiding irrelevant or duplicate content (via `robots.txt`), leading to a more efficient and accurate index of the site. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The portal includes a registration form for new contributors. The developer must decide whether to use client-side scripting or server-side scripting for data validation.

Identify **two** benefits of performing validation on the client side and **two** benefits of performing it on the server side.
parts.markscheme:
### Client-side validation benefits
- **Immediate feedback**: Users receive error messages instantly (e.g., while typing) without waiting for a page reload. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Reduced server load**: Data is only sent to the server once it is correctly formatted, saving bandwidth and processing power. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Server-side validation benefits
- **Security**: Client-side scripts can be bypassed or disabled by a malicious user; server-side validation is the final line of defense to prevent SQL injection or malicious data entry. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Data Integrity/Database checks**: Only the server can check for complex requirements, such as whether a username is already taken in the database. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] per benefit identified, max [2] for client-side and max [2] for server-side.</note>

parts.content:
Search engines may use a ranking algorithm (e.g., PageRank) to estimate the importance of pages when ordering search results.

Explain how a page’s ranking can be influenced by links from other pages.
parts.markscheme:
### Link-based ranking (e.g., PageRank)

- The ranking of a page can increase when **other pages link to it** (incoming links are treated as “votes” or endorsements). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A link from a page that is itself **highly ranked/important** contributes more to the target page’s rank than a link from a low-ranked page. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pages with **more (or stronger) inbound links** tend to be ranked higher because they are interpreted as more authoritative/important. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Rank can be computed by **iteratively distributing** a page’s rank across its outgoing links (so rank “flows” through the web graph). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: The World Wide Web relies on technologies such as URLs and hyperlinks. Define the purpose of a URL in the context of accessing resources on the web.
parts.markscheme:
- A URL uniquely identifies the location/address of a resource on a network/web (e.g., a web page or file) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It allows a client/browser to request and retrieve that specific resource from a web server (including the protocol/domain/path) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two ways in which search engine optimization (SEO) techniques can improve the ranking of a website in search results.
parts.markscheme:
- Using relevant keywords in high-priority areas like page titles, headers, and meta-tags to match common user queries <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Generating high-quality backlinks from other reputable websites to increase the site's authority or "page rank" <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the interaction between a web client and a web server during an HTTP session, specifically detailing the function of a GET request and a response code.
parts.markscheme:
- The client browser sends an HTTP GET request to the server specifying a URL/resource to be retrieved <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The server processes the request and identifies the resource (or an error) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The server returns an HTTP response containing a status code (e.g., 200 OK for success, or 404 Not Found for missing pages) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The response body carries the requested content (such as an HTML document) back to the client for display <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of server-side scripting when a user submits a search query on a travel website to find available hotels. You should make reference to any external software or systems involved.
parts.markscheme:
- A server-side language (such as PHP, Python, or Ruby) receives the query parameters from the client-side form <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The script connects to a database management system (using SQL) to filter and retrieve the specific hotel data requested by the user <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The script then dynamically constructs an HTML page containing the retrieved results to be sent back to the client's browser <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify two reasons why a website handling financial transactions must use the HTTPS protocol instead of standard HTTP.
parts.markscheme:
- HTTPS uses encryption (via SSL/TLS) to ensure that sensitive data like credit card numbers cannot be intercepted and read by third parties <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It uses digital certificates to provide authentication, giving the user confidence that they are communicating with the genuine server and not a malicious imposter <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the process by which a search engine spider (crawler) traverses the web and how this leads to the creation of a search index.
parts.markscheme:
- The spider begins with a seed list of URLs and visits these pages to download and analyze their content <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It follows the hyperlinks found on those pages to discover new URLs, effectively "crawling" from page to page <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The content of each page is parsed to identify keywords, metadata, and structural importance (e.g., headings vs body text) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This processed data is stored in a massive database called an index, which allows the search engine to provide rapid results for user queries without searching the live web in real-time <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: The Web has evolved significantly since its inception, moving from Web 1.0 to Web 3.0.

parts.content: State one characteristic of Web 1.0.
parts.markscheme: - Web 1.0 consisted largely of static, read-only pages with limited user interaction <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how Web 2.0 differs from Web 1.0.
parts.markscheme:
- Web 2.0 introduced user-generated content and social interaction, whereas Web 1.0 was primarily for consuming content <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Technologies such as AJAX allow Web 2.0 to provide dynamic content updates in real-time, unlike the static nature of Web 1.0 <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two challenges associated with implementing Web 3.0.
parts.markscheme:
- Privacy concerns: the use of AI for personalization in Web 3.0 requires processing large amounts of personal data, which can lead to privacy risks <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Development costs: implementing semantic web technologies is resource-intensive and may be too expensive for smaller companies or developers <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Compatibility challenges: it is difficult to ensure that semantic metadata and features are interpreted consistently across all diverse browsers and devices <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Infrastructure requirements: the complexity of Web 3.0 content and its dynamic generation results in much higher data storage and processing needs <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Award marks for any two of the challenges listed above. For each challenge, award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying the challenge and <marks>1 mark</marks> for a corresponding outline/explanation.</note>

questions.specification: A large e-commerce company, ShopMax, operates globally and receives millions of customer visits daily. The website must remain secure, responsive, and accessible from any location worldwide. When customers access the ShopMax website at https://shopmax.com, their web browser communicates with the server hosting the site.

parts.content: State the protocol used in the URL https://shopmax.com.
parts.markscheme:
- State the protocol name: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the URL structure
steps.content:
In IB Computer Science, specifically in **Topic 3.1.9 (Importance of Protocols)** and **Topic C.1.4 (Uniform Resource Identifier and URL)**, we learn that a URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is composed of several parts. 

The standard structure for the beginning of a URL is:

$$\text{protocol} : // \text{domain name}$$

To find the protocol using the **Direct Identification** method, we simply look for the text prefix that comes immediately before the colon and the two forward slashes ($://$).

highlights.text: https://shopmax.com

steps.title: Extract the protocol prefix
steps.content:
Looking at the URL provided in the question:

$$\mathbf{https}://shopmax.com$$

By identifying the string located before the $://$, we can see that the protocol is **HTTPS**.

highlights.text: https://shopmax.com

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The protocol is **HTTPS**, which stands for **Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure**. 

In an IB exam, stating either the acronym or the full name is typically sufficient for the mark, but providing both ensures total clarity.

**Final Answer:**
HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure)

highlights.text: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure)

parts.content: Explain two reasons why HTTPS is used instead of HTTP for the ShopMax website.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that HTTPS provides encryption for data transmitted between the user and the server <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that this ensures sensitive customer data, such as login details or credit card information, is protected from interception by unauthorized parties <marks>R1</marks>
- Identify that HTTPS verifies the authenticity of ShopMax's server through SSL/TLS certificates <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that this prevents phishing attacks or man-in-the-middle attacks by ensuring the user is connecting to the legitimate site <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Analyze Data Encryption
steps.content:
The first major security feature of HTTPS is **encryption**. Unlike HTTP, which sends data in plain text, HTTPS encrypts the data packets sent between the user's browser and the ShopMax server.

This ensures **confidentiality**. Even if a hacker intercepts the data while it travels across the network, they will only see scrambled, unreadable characters. For an e-commerce site like ShopMax, this is essential to protect sensitive information such as login credentials and credit card numbers.

This concept relates to **Topic 3.1.11: Network Security Methods** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: HTTPS provides encryption for data transmitted between the user and the server
sensitive customer data, such as login details or credit card information, is protected from interception by unauthorized parties

steps.title: Analyze Server Authentication
steps.content:
The second key feature is **server authentication**. HTTPS uses SSL/TLS digital certificates to prove that the server the user is communicating with actually belongs to ShopMax.

This builds **trust** and prevents security threats like "Man-in-the-Middle" (MitM) or phishing attacks. Without this certificate, a malicious actor could pretend to be the ShopMax website to steal user data. The certificate verifies that the connection is legitimate before any data is exchanged.

Review **Topic 3.1.9: Importance of Protocols** to understand how these rules govern secure communication.

highlights.text: HTTPS verifies the authenticity of ShopMax's server through SSL/TLS certificates
prevents phishing attacks or man-in-the-middle attacks by ensuring the user is connecting to the legitimate site

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, you need to provide both the identification of the feature and the explanation of its purpose:

1.  **Identification (1 mark):** HTTPS provides encryption.
2.  **Reason (1 mark):** Protects sensitive data (like credit cards) from interception.
3.  **Identification (1 mark):** HTTPS provides server authentication via SSL/TLS certificates.
4.  **Reason (1 mark):** Ensures the user is on the legitimate site, preventing phishing or MitM attacks.

parts.content: Describe the steps a Domain Name Server (DNS) takes to locate the IP address of shopmax.com when a customer enters this URL into their browser.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that the DNS server receives the domain name "shopmax.com" and first checks its local cache for an existing IP address mapping <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe how the DNS server queries other DNS servers hierarchically (root servers $\rightarrow$ .com TLD servers $\rightarrow$ authoritative servers) if the address is not found in the cache <marks>A1</marks>
- State that the DNS resolves the domain name to the corresponding IP address and returns it to the user's browser to establish a connection <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>All three steps must be in the correct logical sequence for full marks.</note>

steps.title: Check the Local Cache
steps.content: When a customer enters $https://shopmax.com$, the DNS server (usually provided by the ISP) first receives the domain name request. Its very first task is to check its own **local cache**. This cache stores the IP addresses of recently visited websites to speed up the process. If the IP for $shopmax.com$ is already there, it can be returned immediately without further searching.

highlights.text: shopmax.com
DNS server receives the domain name "shopmax.com" and first checks its local cache for an existing IP address mapping

steps.title: Hierarchical Server Query
steps.content:
If the IP address is not found in the local cache, the DNS server begins a **sequential resolution process** through the global DNS hierarchy:

1.  **Root Servers**: It first queries a root server, which identifies the Top-Level Domain (TLD) and points toward the $.com$ servers.
2.  **TLD Servers**: It then queries the $.com$ TLD server, which knows which specific authoritative server manages $shopmax.com$.
3.  **Authoritative Servers**: Finally, it reaches the authoritative DNS server for ShopMax, which holds the master record for that domain.

highlights.text: DNS server queries other DNS servers hierarchically (root servers $\rightarrow$ .com TLD servers $\rightarrow$ authoritative servers)

steps.title: Resolve and Return IP
steps.content: Once the authoritative server identifies the correct IP address (e.g., $192.0.2.1$), it sends this information back to the DNS resolver. The DNS resolver then **returns the IP address to the user's browser**. This allows the browser to establish a direct connection with the web server hosting the ShopMax website.

highlights.text: resolves the domain name to the corresponding IP address and returns it to the user's browser

parts.content: ShopMax’s web page content is designed using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Explain the role of each of these technologies in creating a responsive and user-friendly website.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain the role of HTML: It provides the structure of the web page by organizing content into elements such as headings, paragraphs, and images <marks>A1</marks>
- Define the layout: It establishes the essential content and semantic meaning of the page <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain the role of CSS: It controls the visual appearance, including colors, fonts, and styling, to ensure a consistent brand look <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe responsive design: It enables the website to adapt its layout automatically for different screen sizes and devices <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain the role of JavaScript: It enables interactivity by allowing dynamic updates to the page content without requiring a full reload <marks>A1</marks>
- Implement features: It facilitates complex functionality such as shopping carts, form validation, or personalized user suggestions <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Introduction to Web Technologies
steps.content: To understand how ShopMax creates a great user experience, we look at the 'three-layer model' of web design. Each technology—HTML, CSS, and JavaScript—has a distinct responsibility. This topic is covered in **Topic C.1: Creating the Web**. Let's break them down one by one.

steps.title: HTML: The Structural Layer
steps.content:
HTML (HyperText Markup Language) acts as the skeleton of the ShopMax website. 

*   **Role**: It organizes content into logical elements like headings (h1, h2), paragraphs (p), and images (img). 
*   **Impact**: It provides the **semantic meaning** of the page. This is crucial for accessibility; for example, screen readers use HTML tags to help visually impaired users navigate the product catalog. Without HTML, the browser wouldn't know which text is a product title and which is a price.

highlights.text: organizing content into elements such as headings, paragraphs, and images

steps.title: CSS: The Presentation Layer
steps.content:
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the 'skin' or the visual design layer that makes the site look professional.

*   **Role**: It controls the visual appearance, including brand colors, fonts, and the overall layout. 
*   **Responsive Design**: CSS is the primary tool for making a site **responsive**. Using techniques like 'media queries,' CSS allows the ShopMax layout to automatically adapt from a wide desktop screen to a narrow smartphone screen, ensuring the site is usable everywhere.

highlights.text: visual appearance, including colors, fonts, and styling

steps.title: JavaScript: The Behavioral Layer
steps.content:
JavaScript is the 'muscle' of the site, providing the logic and interactivity.

*   **Role**: It enables **interactivity** by allowing the page to change dynamically without needing a full page reload (client-side scripting).
*   **Features**: For a company like ShopMax, JavaScript handles complex tasks like updating the shopping cart total in real-time, validating email addresses in checkout forms, or providing search suggestions as the user types. This makes the site feel fast and responsive to user actions.

highlights.text: enables interactivity by allowing dynamic updates to the page content

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To get full marks on this 6-mark question, ensure you have provided two clear points for each technology:

1.  **HTML**: Mention content organization (1 mark) and semantic structure/layout (1 mark).
2.  **CSS**: Mention visual branding/styling (1 mark) and its role in responsive design/adaptation (1 mark).
3.  **JavaScript**: Mention dynamic interactivity/updates (1 mark) and specific functional implementations like carts or forms (1 mark).

steps.title: The Building Analogy
steps.content:
To understand how these three technologies work together for **ShopMax**, imagine you are building a modern, smart-tech house. 

*   **HTML** is the wooden frame and the foundation (the structure).
*   **CSS** is the paint, the wallpaper, and the ability for the house to change its layout based on the size of the lot (the aesthetics).
*   **JavaScript** is the smart-home system, the plumbing, and the electric doors (the functionality).

Let's break down how each one earns you marks in an IB exam.

steps.title: HTML: The Structural Foundation
steps.content:
Just as a house needs a frame to hold the roof, **HTML** (HyperText Markup Language) provides the essential structure of the web page. 

*   **Role:** It organizes content into specific elements like headings, paragraphs, and images. Without HTML, the browser wouldn't know which text is a product title and which is a price.
*   **Semantic Meaning:** It establishes the layout and the "meaning" of the page (e.g., using a `<nav>` tag for a navigation menu), ensuring the content is logically ordered for both users and search engines.

highlights.text: HTML

steps.title: CSS: Style and Responsiveness
steps.content:
If HTML is the frame, **CSS** (Cascading Style Sheets) is the interior design. It makes the **ShopMax** site look professional and adapt to the user.

*   **Visual Appearance:** CSS controls the fonts, colors, and overall branding. This ensures that every page a customer visits has a consistent "ShopMax" look.
*   **Responsive Design:** This is a key IB concept. CSS allows the website to be "responsive," meaning it automatically adjusts the layout for different screen sizes (like shrinking a 3-column desktop layout into a 1-column mobile layout).

highlights.text: CSS

steps.title: JavaScript: Interactivity and Function
steps.content:
Finally, **JavaScript** is what makes the house "smart." It handles the actions and behavior of the site.

*   **Dynamic Interactivity:** JavaScript allows the page to update content *without* needing to refresh the whole page. For example, when you click "Add to Cart," the cart icon might update instantly.
*   **Complex Features:** It facilitates the advanced logic needed for a global e-commerce site, such as validating a credit card form, providing personalized product suggestions, or running the checkout process.

highlights.text: JavaScript

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To get the full 6 marks, ensure your explanation covers two points for each technology:

1.  **HTML:** Structure/Elements and Semantic meaning/Layout.
2.  **CSS:** Visual styling/Branding and Responsive design/Adaptability.
3.  **JavaScript:** Dynamic updates/Interactivity and Complex functionality (like shopping carts).

This topic is covered in **Topic C.1: Creating the Web**. Reviewing the difference between client-side and server-side scripting in that chapter will help you understand why JavaScript is so powerful!

steps.title: HTML: Building the Foundation
steps.content:
HTML (HyperText Markup Language) serves as the **skeleton** of the ShopMax website. From a user-centric perspective, its primary role is to organize content so that it is readable and logically structured. * **Structure and Organization:** HTML uses tags to define elements like `

` for headings or `` for product photos), HTML provides the essential content and meaning that screen readers and search engines use to make the site accessible and findable for all users.

highlights.text: organizing content into elements such as headings, paragraphs, and images

steps.title: CSS: Style and Responsiveness
steps.content:
While HTML provides the content, CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) focuses on the **visual experience** and how that content adapts to different devices (responsiveness).

*   **Visual Appearance:** CSS controls the branding of ShopMax, defining the colors, typography, and spacing. This creates a professional and user-friendly interface that builds trust with the customer.
*   **Responsive Design:** Using techniques like *media queries*, CSS allows the website to detect the user's screen size. For example, a three-column product grid on a desktop might automatically stack into a single column on a smartphone, ensuring the site remains usable and easy to navigate regardless of the device used.

highlights.text: adapt its layout automatically for different screen sizes and devices

steps.title: JavaScript: Dynamic Interaction
steps.content:
JavaScript provides the **behavior** of the website, making it feel alive and responsive to user actions.

*   **Dynamic Updates:** Instead of waiting for a whole page to reload every time a user adds an item to their cart, JavaScript allows the page to update specific sections instantly. This creates a much smoother, faster user experience.
*   **Complex Functionality:** JavaScript handles the "heavy lifting" of user interaction, such as validating that an email address is entered correctly in a form, providing real-time search suggestions, or managing the complex logic required for a global shopping cart system.

highlights.text: enables interactivity by allowing dynamic updates to the page content without requiring a full reload

steps.title: Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure all 6 marks on an IB exam, ensure you connect each technology to its specific function:

1.  **HTML:** Structure (headings/images) and Semantic layout.
2.  **CSS:** Visual styling and Responsive adaptation for different screens.
3.  **JavaScript:** Interactivity (dynamic updates) and functional features (shopping carts/validation).

questions.specification: A software engineering team is designing a library management system with objects representing $\text{Book}$, $\text{Member}$, and $\text{Transaction}$.

parts.content: Construct a basic $\text{UML}$ class diagram to represent the $\text{Book}$ object with at least two attributes and one method.

- **Include** at least two attributes in the middle compartment (e.g., $\text{title: String}$, $\text{author: String}$, $\text{ISBN: String}$, $\text{pages: Integer}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Include** at least one method in the bottom compartment (e.g., $\text{checkOut()}$, $\text{returnBook()}$, $\text{getDetails()}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A non-profit organization, OpenResearch, publishes studies and data sets on a freely accessible website to encourage global collaboration in scientific research.

parts.content: The OpenResearch website is accessible via URLs that provide direct links to various datasets (e.g., $\text{https://openresearch.org/dataset1}$). Describe two benefits of using URLs to make these resources available.
parts.markscheme:
- URLs enable direct linking (deep-linking) to specific datasets <marks>R1</marks>
- This improves accessibility as users do not need to navigate from the homepage to find content <marks>A1</marks>
- URLs can be designed with a logical, human-readable hierarchy <marks>R1</marks>
- This allows researchers to easily predict or remember the location of related datasets <marks>A1</marks>
- URLs can be indexed by search engines <marks>R1</marks>
- This increases the visibility and discoverability of the research by making it searchable via external web tools <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award marks for any two of the above points or other valid benefits. Each point is worth up to 2 marks.</note>

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content:
In this question, we are looking at how **URLs (Uniform Resource Locators)** improve the way researchers interact with the OpenResearch website. Instead of just having one address for the whole site, every piece of data has its own unique path. This is a core concept in **Topic C.1.5: Purpose of a URL**.

We need to identify two distinct benefits and explain them to earn all 4 marks (2 marks per benefit).

highlights.text: https://openresearch.org/dataset1

steps.title: Benefit 1: Deep-Linking Efficiency
steps.content:
The most significant advantage is **deep-linking**. By providing a specific URL for a dataset, the organization allows users to bypass the homepage entirely.

**Explanation:** Researchers often know exactly which dataset they need. Providing a direct link means they don't have to navigate through multiple menus, search bars, or categories on the main site. This saves significant time and reduces the cognitive load required to find resources.

highlights.text: URLs enable direct linking (deep-linking) to specific datasets
users do not need to navigate from the homepage to find content

steps.title: Benefit 2: Search Engine Visibility
steps.content:
Another major benefit is that unique URLs allow for **external indexing** by search engines like Google or specialized academic tools.

**Explanation:** When each dataset has its own URL, search engine "crawlers" can find and index them individually. This means a scientist looking for specific data on "Climate Trends 2023" might find the direct link in their search results without ever having visited the OpenResearch homepage first. This greatly increases the **discoverability** of the research.

highlights.text: URLs can be indexed by search engines
increases the visibility and discoverability

steps.title: Review Mark Allocation
steps.content:
To ensure you get the full 4 marks, check that your answer provides both a **Reason (R)** and an **Application/Explanation (A)** for each point:

1.  **Direct Linking (R1):** Bypasses the homepage to save user time **(A1)**.
2.  **Indexing (R1):** Allows search engines to find specific pages, increasing global reach **(A1)**.

Alternatively, you could mention that a **logical hierarchy** in URLs (e.g., $\text{/biology/genetics/dataset1}$) makes the location of related data predictable and easy to remember for researchers.

steps.title: Understand the role of URLs
steps.content: In IB Computer Science, specifically in **Topic C: Web Science**, a URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is defined as the global address of documents and other resources on the World Wide Web. For an organization like OpenResearch, using specific URLs like `https://openresearch.org/dataset1` is crucial for making data accessible to the global scientific community.

highlights.text: https://openresearch.org/dataset1

steps.title: Benefit 1: External Discoverability
steps.content:
The primary benefit of using unique URLs is **Search Engine Indexing**. When each dataset has its own URL, web crawlers (used by search engines like Google or Bing) can find and index these specific pages individually. 

This significantly increases the **discoverability** of the research. Instead of a user needing to know that OpenResearch exists, they can simply search for keywords related to "dataset 1" on an external search engine and be directed straight to the resource.

steps.title: Benefit 2: Direct Deep-Linking
steps.content:
URLs allow for **deep-linking**, which means providing a direct path to a specific piece of content rather than just the homepage. 

This improves **accessibility** and efficiency for researchers. They don't have to navigate through complex menus or use an internal search bar on the OpenResearch site. They can bookmark the link or share it directly in citations, allowing others to access the exact data set with one click.

steps.title: Benefit 3: Human-Readable Hierarchy
steps.content:
URLs can be designed with a **logical, human-readable hierarchy** (e.g., `/biology/2023/dataset1`). This makes the links "semantic," meaning the address itself gives the user information about what the resource contains.

This helps researchers **predict or remember** where related datasets might be located, improving the overall user experience and trust in the digital archive.

steps.title: Final Mark Allocation
steps.content:
To earn the full 4 marks, you need to describe **two** benefits. Each benefit is worth 2 marks: one mark for identifying the technical reason ($R1$) and one mark for explaining the resulting advantage or application ($A1$).

**Example Answer:**
1. **Indexing ($R1$):** Unique URLs allow search engines to crawl and index specific datasets. **Benefit ($A1$):** This increases global discoverability via external search tools.
2. **Deep-linking ($R1$):** URLs provide direct access to specific data. **Benefit ($A1$):** This improves accessibility as users don't need to navigate from the homepage.

steps.title: Identify the Goal
steps.content:
The question asks for **two benefits** of using URLs (Uniform Resource Locators) for a research organization's website. We need to explain these benefits in a way that shows how they help users access and organize information efficiently.

This topic is covered in **Option C: Web Science**, specifically in **C.1.4 (Uniform Resource Identifier and URL)** and **C.1.5 (Purpose of a URL)**.

steps.title: Benefit 1: Logical Hierarchy
steps.content:
One major benefit of URLs is that they can be designed with a **human-readable, logical hierarchy**. 

Instead of a random string of numbers, a URL can reflect the folder structure of the data. For example, if a researcher is at $\text{https://openresearch.org/biology/dataset1}$, they can easily predict that the second dataset is at $\text{https://openresearch.org/biology/dataset2}$. This consistent structure makes links easy to **memorize** and allows researchers to **predict** where related data is located without needing to search for it every time.

highlights.text: https://openresearch.org/dataset1

steps.title: Benefit 2: Direct Linking
steps.content:
URLs enable **direct linking** (also known as deep-linking). This means a user can go straight to a specific resource, like a CSV file or a PDF report, using a unique address. 

This significantly improves **accessibility** and efficiency because researchers don't have to navigate through the homepage or multiple menus to find what they need. They can bookmark the exact dataset or share it directly with colleagues via email or social media.

highlights.text: direct links to various datasets

steps.title: Final Summary of Marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks by stating the feature of the URL and then explaining the advantage it provides to the user:

*   **Benefit 1 (2 Marks):** URLs can have a logical/human-readable hierarchy (1), which helps users predict or remember the location of related research data (1).
*   **Benefit 2 (2 Marks):** URLs allow for direct/deep-linking to specific datasets (1), which improves accessibility by removing the need to navigate through a homepage (1).

Other valid points include **search engine indexing**, which makes the research more discoverable to the global scientific community.

parts.content: The Domain Name System (DNS) is used to resolve requests to OpenResearch's servers. Explain two advantages of using DNS in accessing OpenResearch’s resources.
parts.markscheme:
- DNS allows users to access the site using memorable domain names instead of complex IP addresses <marks>R1</marks>
- This makes the resources more accessible and reduces the chance of input errors compared to numerical addresses <marks>A1</marks>
- DNS allows for updates to server IP addresses without changing the domain name <marks>R1</marks>
- This reduces maintenance complexity and ensures existing external links remain functional during server migrations <marks>A1</marks>
- DNS can facilitate load balancing by mapping a domain to multiple servers <marks>R1</marks>
- This improves site performance and reliability by efficiently handling many simultaneous requests <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award marks for any two of the above points or other valid advantages. Each point is worth up to 2 marks.</note>

steps.title: Understand DNS function
steps.content:
To answer this question, we first need to recall the primary role of the **Domain Name System (DNS)**. In networking, computers identify each other using numerical **IP addresses** (like $192.0.2.1$). However, humans find it difficult to remember these strings of numbers. 

DNS acts like a phonebook for the internet, translating human-readable domain names (like `openresearch.org`) into the machine-readable IP addresses required to route data. This concept is covered in **Topic 3: Networks** and **Option C: Web Science**.

steps.title: Advantage 1: User accessibility
steps.content:
The first advantage is the use of **mnemonics** (memorable names). 

**Identification:** DNS allows users to access OpenResearch's site using a memorable domain name instead of a complex, numerical IP address. 

**Explanation:** Since OpenResearch aims to "encourage global collaboration," having an easy-to-remember name makes their resources more accessible to researchers worldwide. It also significantly reduces the chance of **input errors**, as users are less likely to mistype a name than a long sequence of numbers.

highlights.text: encourage global collaboration
DNS allows users to access the site using memorable domain names instead of complex IP addresses

steps.title: Advantage 2: Infrastructure flexibility
steps.content:
The second advantage is the **abstraction** of the server's physical location (IP address) from its identity (Domain Name).

**Identification:** DNS allows OpenResearch to update their server’s IP address without changing the domain name itself.

**Explanation:** If the organization needs to migrate to a more powerful server or change service providers to handle more data sets, they only need to update the DNS record. This ensures that all existing **external links** in scientific papers or bookmarks remain functional, providing a seamless experience for the research community and reducing maintenance complexity.

highlights.text: DNS allows for updates to server IP addresses without changing the domain name
ensures existing external links remain functional during server migrations

steps.title: Structure your response
steps.content:
When writing your answer for the IB exam, ensure you clearly state the technical advantage and then develop it by explaining the benefit to the organization. 

Each of the two points is worth **2 marks**: 
*   **1 mark** for identifying a valid advantage (the "Reason").
*   **1 mark** for the developed explanation (the "Application").

Following this structure ensures you meet the criteria for the full 4 marks.

parts.content: Explain one potential risk of providing open access to research datasets and suggest a strategy OpenResearch could use to mitigate this risk.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify a risk: The potential for data to be misinterpreted, misused, or used out of context by third parties <marks>R1</marks>
- Suggest a mitigation strategy: Providing comprehensive metadata and documentation alongside the datasets <marks>R1</marks>
- Explain the mitigation: This provides researchers with the necessary context and methodology to ensure the data is applied accurately and ethically <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept other valid risks (e.g., privacy concerns, data integrity) and appropriate mitigation strategies.</note>

questions.specification: _GlobalPulse_ is a digital news agency that has recently launched an interactive news portal. The portal allows users to search for articles, log into personal accounts, and view multimedia content. To ensure the site is accessible and performant, the developers followed strict W3C web standards and optimized the site for search engine discovery.

parts.content:
A user navigates to the following URL to read an article:
`https://www.globalpulse.com/archive/today/report.html` 

Identify **two** specific components of this URL.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the protocol (HTTPS) <marks>1</marks>
- **Identify** the domain name / host (www.globalpulse.com) <marks>1</marks>
- **Identify** the path or directory (/archive/today/) <marks>1</marks>
- **Identify** the file name or resource (report.html) <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for each component identified up to 2 marks.</note>

parts.content: Distinguish between the roles of client-side scripting and server-side scripting when a user logs into their account on the _GlobalPulse_ portal.
parts.markscheme:
### Client-side Scripting
- **Role**: Handles tasks within the user's browser, such as initial form validation (e.g., checking if the email field is empty) before the data is sent to the server <marks>1</marks>
- **Benefit**: Provides immediate feedback to the user and reduces unnecessary traffic/load on the server <marks>1</marks>

### Server-side Scripting
- **Role**: Handles sensitive tasks on the web server, such as querying the database to verify the username and password <marks>1</marks>
- **Benefit**: Ensures security because the script logic and database credentials are not visible to the user, and it can generate dynamic content based on the user's profile <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Discuss the importance of metadata and site hierarchy for web crawlers (spiders) to ensure the news portal is indexed effectively.
parts.markscheme:
### Importance of Metadata
- **Meta Tags**: Use of keywords and descriptions in the HTML head allows crawlers to categorize the page content accurately for search results <marks>1</marks>
- **Alt Text**: Providing alternative text for images allows crawlers (which cannot 'see' images) to understand the visual context of the news report <marks>1</marks>
- **Header Tags**: Proper use of $$<h1>$$ to $$<h6>$$ tags provides a semantic structure that tells the crawler which topics are most relevant on the page <marks>1</marks>

### Site Hierarchy and Structure
- **Sitemaps**: Providing an XML sitemap helps the crawler discover all the pages on the portal, including deep-linked archives that might not be easily reachable via the homepage <marks>1</marks>
- **Internal Linking**: A logical link structure (e.g., categorizing by 'World', 'Tech', 'Sports') helps the crawler map the relationship between different topics <marks>1</marks>
- **Robots.txt**: This file directs the crawler on which parts of the site to index and which to ignore (e.g., private user account pages), optimizing the crawler's 'crawl budget' <marks>1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for each point discussed up to 6 marks. For full marks, the response should address both metadata and site structure/hierarchy.</note>

parts.content: Outline the steps of the HTTP request-response cycle that occur when a user's browser requests the homepage of _GlobalPulse_.
parts.markscheme:
- **Step 1**: The client (browser) sends an HTTP request message to the server (e.g., a GET request for index.html), which includes headers like the user-agent and accepted file types <marks>1</marks>
- **Step 2**: The web server processes the request, locates the requested resource (or executes a script to generate it), and prepares a response <marks>1</marks>
- **Step 3**: The server sends an HTTP response message back to the client, containing a status code (e.g., 200 OK) and the content of the requested page <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

questions.specification: The evolution of the World Wide Web is supported by a set of protocols and standards that ensure global compatibility and information retrieval. While often used interchangeably, the technical distinction between the underlying infrastructure and the services running on it is fundamental to web science.

parts.content: Distinguish between the physical infrastructure of the Internet and the software-based service known as the World Wide Web.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** the Internet as the global system of interconnected computer networks (hardware, cables, routers) that use the TCP/IP protocol suite <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Define** the World Wide Web as a service/application that runs on the Internet, consisting of a collection of multimedia documents linked by hyperlinks and URLs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that the Internet acts as the carrier or medium, whereas the Web is just one way of accessing information over that medium (alongside email or File Transfer Protocol) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the sequence of events that occurs during a standard Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) "GET" request cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that the client (browser) sends a request header to the server, specifying the resource path and the method (GET) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Describe** how the server receives the request, locates the specific file or resource on its storage, and processes any necessary scripts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that the server sends a response back to the client, which includes a status code (e.g., 200 OK) followed by the actual content/payload of the requested file <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Search engines use automated programs to manage the vast amount of data available on the web. Describe the process these programs use to discover, categorize, and rank information.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** the process of crawling, where "spiders" or bots follow hyperlinks from one page to another to discover new or updated content <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Describe** the indexing phase, where the discovered data is analyzed (keywords, metadata, structure) and stored in a massive database for quick retrieval <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** that search engines use proprietary algorithms to rank pages based on relevance and authority <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identify** factors such as the frequency of keywords, the quality of backlinks, or the mobile-friendliness of the site that influence the ranking <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that when a user enters a query, the search engine searches its index (not the live web) to provide a list of results in milliseconds <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award up to 5 marks max.</note>

parts.content: Explain the importance of the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) standards in the development of modern web applications.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that W3C standards ensure interoperability, allowing web pages to look and function consistently across different browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox) and devices <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that these standards promote accessibility (WCAG), ensuring that the web remains usable for people with disabilities or those using assistive technologies <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Outline** that by following open standards, developers avoid proprietary "lock-in," ensuring the web remains a decentralized and open platform that is future-proofed against technology changes <marks>1 mark</marks>
